articles.author: Jon Gambrell, Sam Mednick, David Rising
articles.description: Iran is making threats against tourist and recreational destinations worldwide, saying it will deny its enemies their security while insisting that it is still building missiles nearly three weeks into the war. U.S.-Israeli strikes have killed a slew of Tehran’s top leaders and hammered its weapons and energy industries. Iran fired on Israel and energy sites in neighboring Gulf Arab states Friday. With little information coming out of Iran, it was not clear how much damage its arms, nuclear or energy facilities have sustained since the war began Feb. 28 or even who was truly in charge of the country. But Iran has showed it is still capable of attacks that are choking off oil supplies and denting the global economy.
articles.title: Iran is defiant nearly 3 weeks into the war

articles.author: Brian P. D. Hannon, Sylvia Hui, Luena Rodriguez-Feo Vileira, Michael Warren, Nell Clark, Chris Blake
articles.description: U.S. President Donald Trump says he is considering ‘winding down’ the Middle East military operation, but his statement seemed at odds with his administration’s move to send more troops and warships to the region.
articles.title: Trump says he is considering ‘winding down’ Middle East military operation

articles.author: Bassem Mroue
articles.description: An Israeli strike has hit a Hezbollah-run health center in southern Lebanon and killed 12 medical workers, as Israel widens its campaign beyond fighters. Like in previous wars, Israel is going after Hezbollah's civilians wings in an attempt to crush the Iran-backed group once and for all. The Israeli strikes have hit Hezbollah-linked civilian institutions, including clinics, low-price shops, and the group’s financial network. Hezbollah leader Naim Kassem calls it an existential battle and rejects surrender. Lebanese officials reportedly hear Israel wants Hezbollah to disarm nationwide. Amnesty International says strikes tied to the financial arm need war-crimes investigations.
articles.title: Israel strikes Hezbollah's civilian and military wings in attempt to crush group

articles.author: Tiffany Stanley
articles.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth brings Christian language into the Pentagon as the U.S. and Israel wage war against Iran. He has called Iran’s leaders “religious fanatics” and quoted a biblical Psalm about war. Critics warn his frequent Christian rhetoric can fuel Iranian hostility and strain the military’s secular role. Hegseth has also defended the Crusades and has crusader-themed tattoos. A watchdog group claims U.S. commanders frame the war as biblical prophecy, but The Associated Press cannot verify those allegations. Hegseth has pushed policy changes that reflect his Christian views, including a revamp of the military chaplain corps.
articles.title: Hegseth’s Christian rhetoric draws renewed scrutiny during Iran war

articles.author: Kevin Freking
articles.description: A bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security has failed to advance in the Senate amid growing concerns about long lines to get through screening at some of the country’s biggest airports. Democrats declined to provide the support needed to move the funding measure toward final passage as they seek changes at those DHS agencies responsible for immigration enforcement. Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer said he would offer an alternative measure Saturday to fund just the Transportation Security Administration, which screens passengers and luggage for hazardous items. That too is likely to fail as lawmakers hold a rare weekend session.
articles.title: Bill to fund Homeland Security fails again despite airport lines

articles.description: President Donald Trump has issued an executive order barring College Football Playoff and other postseason games from airing during the annual Army-Navy matchup in December. Trump directed the commerce secretary and the FCC chairman to coordinate with the playoff committee, the NCAA and media rights partners to ensure an exclusive broadcasting window for the storied rivalry traditionally played the second Saturday each December. Trump’s order makes reference to potential expansion of the CFP that likely would require an earlier start for the playoff. In the first two years of the 12-team format, the first-round games were the weekend after Army-Navy.
articles.title: Trump's executive order bars CFP from conflicts with Army-Navy game

articles.author: David Bauder
articles.description: A judge this week ruled that Voice of America's employees — and the news organization itself — get back to work. Whether that actually happens is anybody's guess. The government-run agency that broadcasts news around the world has been limping along with a skeleton staff for the past year, largely shut down by the Trump administration. But twice in the past two weeks, U.S. District Court Judge Royce C. Lamberth has ruled in favor of lawsuits designed to keep it alive, saying that its overseer didn't have the authority to take the actions she did, and that employees placed on paid leave should return to work.
articles.title: Can Voice of America be put back together again?

articles.author: Matthew Lee
articles.description: The State Department is setting up 12 regional hubs to coordinate disaster and emergency humanitarian responses under the auspices of a new bureau that will oversee some of the functions that had been handled by the now-dismantled U.S. Agency for International Development. The department says the new Bureau of Disaster and Humanitarian Response will be based in Washington with about 200 staffers. But on-the-ground operations are to be run from the regional hubs, which will be located in Latin America, Asia, Europe and Africa. The Trump administration has been criticized by many aid and relief organizations for closing down USAID, which had been the government’s main platform for providing foreign assistance for decades.
articles.title: US to set up 12 regional disaster response hubs

articles.author: M.k. Wildeman
articles.description: Unusual March heat has pushed temperatures into record territory in parts of the West. More extremes are expected in the days ahead. While heat is most acutely felt by people exposed to it, graphics and charts convey the scale of this extreme event. Graphics show where temperature records may have been broken, where to expect extreme temperatures next week and what kind of heat to expect in April, May and June. An Associated Press analysis found that roughly a quarter of March heat records at 400 weather stations across the United States may be tied or broken this month.
articles.title: Extreme heat across the US in 4 graphics

articles.author: Jennifer Sinco Kelleher, Audrey McAvoy
articles.description: More than 200 people were rescued Friday as heavy rains pummeled the Hawaiian island of Oahu and triggered the worst flooding the island has in 20 years. Hawaii Gov. Josh Green says the damage could top $1 billion. Muddy floodwaters from severe rains have inundated communities and prompted evacuation orders for more than 5,500 people in towns north of Honolulu. Officials are warning about the possible failure of a 120-year-old dam. Officials have been watching dam levels since a storm last week dumped heavy rain across the state, which led to catastrophic flooding that washed away roads and homes.
articles.title: Over 5,500 people told to evacuate floods on Oahu's North Shore
